name: Northwestern Catalan (Lleidatà)
notes: North-Western Catalan (català nord-occidental: lleidatà, pallarès, ribagorçà, tortosí), the northern half of the WESTERN block. Like Valencian and unlike Central Catalan it has NO unstressed vowel reduction to [ə]: the seven-vowel system is maintained unstressed, so ⟨a⟩ stays [a], ⟨e⟩ [e]/[ɛ] and ⟨o⟩ [o]/[ɔ] with no o → [u] raising, and its function words are unreduced (que [ke], de [ðe]) — Recasens 1996; Veny 1982 ch. 3. Its own hallmark is that WORD-FINAL unstressed ⟨-a⟩ opens to [ɛ] (una porta [ˈunɛ ˈpɔɾtɛ], dia [ˈðiɛ]) — the lleidatà final-⟨a⟩, distinct from both the Central [ə] and the Valencian [a]. Unlike Valencian it DELETES word-final ⟨-r⟩ (aixecar [ajʃeˈka], marxar [maɾˈtʃa]) and keeps the Central ⟨v⟩ = /b/ merger (betacism), so it inherits both from Central Catalan, along with final-cluster simplification, spirantization, final devoicing and cross-word voicing assimilation. Western Catalan (Valencian, North-Western) has the affricate [tʃ] for ⟨x⟩ word-initially and after a consonant where the Eastern block has the fricative [ʃ] (xiquet [tʃiˈket], marxar [maɾˈtʃaɾ]), and keeps the glide of ⟨ix⟩ = [jʃ] (caixa [ˈkajʃa]) that Central Catalan absorbs (Wheeler 2005 §5.1; Veny 1982 ch. 3).

sources.author: Wheeler, M. W.
sources.title: The Phonology of Catalan
sources.notes: The reference phonology of Catalan and the primary authority for this spec. Cited by SECTION, not page: §2.3 vowel systems and the unstressed reduction of the Eastern block (reduction is stress-conditioned; the Western block does not reduce); §2.5 the diphthong inventory; §3.1 stress and its orthographic marking; §5.1 the consonant inventory, ⟨c/g⟩ before a front vowel, ⟨x⟩ and word-final ⟨-ig⟩; §5.2 spirantization of /b d ɡ/ after a continuant and the pre-lateral gemination that blocks it; §5.3 final-obstruent devoicing and cross-word voicing assimilation; §10.2–§10.5 the post-lexical/phrase-level processes — final-rhotic loss, final-cluster simplification, nasal place assimilation and phrase-level lenition. Section numbers are given because the printed page numbers were not verified.

sources.author: Recasens, D.
sources.title: Fonètica descriptiva del català: assaig de caracterització de la pronúncia del vocalisme i consonantisme del català al segle XX
sources.notes: The descriptive survey of Catalan vowels and consonants across the dialects: the Western block (Valencian, North-Western) does not reduce unstressed vowels; Balearic reduces ⟨a⟩/⟨e⟩ to [ə] but does NOT raise unstressed ⟨o⟩ to [u]; the North-Western word-final ⟨-a⟩ = [ɛ]. Pages not verified.

sources.author: Veny, J.
sources.title: Els parlars catalans: síntesi de dialectologia
sources.notes: The standard dialectology of Catalan and the source for the isoglosses this spec encodes per dialect: final ⟨-r⟩ retained in Valencian and deleted in the rest; final-cluster simplification after a nasal everywhere but after a lateral only in the Eastern block; Western [tʃ] for initial ⟨x⟩ and [jʃ] for ⟨ix⟩; the lleidatà final ⟨-a⟩ = [ɛ]. Pages not verified.

sources.author: Institut d'Estudis Catalans
sources.title: Gramàtica de la llengua catalana
sources.notes: The normative grammar. Ch. 3 (l'accent) is the source for the stress block: the written accent marks the stressed vowel and always wins; unmarked words ending in a vowel, a vowel+⟨-s⟩ or ⟨-en/-in⟩ are paroxytone and all other endings — including the consonant+⟨s⟩ plurals — are oxytone; the diaeresis ⟨ï ü⟩ marks hiatus, not stress. §6.2 and the 'mots àtons' list are the source for the clitic function words (articles, contractions, atonic prepositions/conjunctions and weak pronouns) that carry no stress and therefore reduce. Cited by chapter; pages not verified.

sources.author: Ladefoged, P. & Maddieson, I.
sources.title: The Sounds of the World's Languages
sources.notes: Cross-linguistic phonetic reference for the segment inventory symbols used here.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Catalan phonology (Wikipedia)
sources.notes: Accessible overview of the vowel and consonant inventories, the Eastern/Western reduction split, spirantization, final devoicing and final-cluster simplification; useful as a cross-check on the primary sources above.

sources.author: Wikipedia contributors
sources.title: Catalan dialects (Wikipedia)
sources.notes: Accessible overview of the Eastern/Western split and of the isoglosses encoded in the four dialect specs.

location.notes: Lleida — the centre of the north-western (Lleidatà) area. [Wikidata Q15090 P625]

stress.notes: Catalan accentuation per the Institut d'Estudis Catalans (IEC) norm (Gramàtica de la llengua catalana, ch. 3; Wheeler 2005 §3.1). A written accent (à è é í ò ó ú) always wins and marks the stressed vowel. Unmarked words are OXYTONE when they end in a consonant other than -s-preceded-by-a-vowel and other than -en/-in — including the consonant+s plurals (-ns, -rs, -ts, -nts, -ps …), which are oxytone because the plural preserves the stress of the singular (important → importants). Unmarked words are PAROXYTONE when they end in a vowel, in a vowel + -s (-as, -es, -is, -os, -us) or in -en/-in (semblen, parlin). The diaeresis ⟨ï ü⟩ marks hiatus/pronounced ⟨u⟩, NOT stress, so it is deliberately absent from marked_vowels (següent is [səˈɣwen], not [səˈɣwent] with stress on ⟨ü⟩). Proparoxytones always carry a written accent, so the unmarked default is paroxytone. The diphthongs list is the Catalan inventory of tautosyllabic vowel sequences (Wheeler 2005 §2.5; IEC grammar ch. 4): the falling diphthongs ⟨ai au ei eu iu oi ou ui⟩ and the rising ⟨ua ue uo ui⟩ after ⟨g/q⟩. Every other vowel sequence is HIATUS and counts as two syllables — te-ni-a, ve-ï-na, di-a — which is what puts the stress, and therefore the unstressed-vowel reduction, on the right vowel.

allophone_rules.notes: Western Catalan (Valencian, North-Western) simplifies a final cluster only after a NASAL, not after a lateral: important [impoɾˈtan] loses its stop, but molt [ˈmolt] and alta [ˈalta] keep theirs, where Central and Balearic Catalan have [ˈmol] (Wheeler 2005 §10.4; Veny 1982 ch. 3; AVL, Gramàtica normativa valenciana, ch. 2). The inherited Central rule's lateral context is dropped rather than the rule itself.

Western Catalan (Valencian, North-Western) simplifies a final cluster only after a NASAL, not after a lateral: important [impoɾˈtan] loses its stop, but molt [ˈmolt] and alta [ˈalta] keep theirs, where Central and Balearic Catalan have [ˈmol] (Wheeler 2005 §10.4; Veny 1982 ch. 3; AVL, Gramàtica normativa valenciana, ch. 2). The inherited Central rule's lateral context is dropped rather than the rule itself.
